But scanners only find what they're built to find: logic errors, missing authorization, and 'technically works, badly wrong' designs sail straight through. GitHub says the same about Copilot code review — it's a supplement and 'not guaranteed to spot all problems.' Layer the machines on top of human review; never swap them in for it.

questions.prompt: Before letting an agent loose on a task, source-control discipline says your first move is:
choices: Disable git integration so the agent cannot make commits attributed to your name
Work directly on main — feature branches reduce velocity, per the velocity dashboard
Start from a clean, committed state on a branch, so every agent change is diffable and revertible
Hold all commits until the work is finished, then commit everything as 'misc changes'
questions.explanation: A clean starting commit on a branch means every line the agent touches shows up in a diff you can review, and any wrong turn is one revert away instead of an archaeology project. Mixing agent changes into an already-dirty working tree — or batching a week of work into one mega-commit — destroys exactly the visibility that makes review possible. Simon Willison lists source-control discipline as a defining practice of agentic engineering, alongside testing and written plans. Git is your undo button; make sure it works before you need it.

questions.prompt: Copilot can review pull requests now. Does that mean AI review can replace your team's human code review?
choices: No — GitHub itself positions its code review as a supplement, 'not guaranteed to spot all problems'
Yes, provided you use a different model to review than the one that wrote the code
Yes, for small PRs — human review is only economically justified above five hundred lines
No, but purely because the audit system requires a human name in the approver field
questions.explanation: AI review is a useful extra pass, but the vendor's own documentation says it is a supplement that is not guaranteed to catch everything — and a second model reviewing the first shares the same blind spots around intent, architecture, and business logic. Size is no shield either; some of the worst bugs ship in three-line diffs. The audit-trail answer gets the conclusion right for the wrong reason: humans review because humans own correctness, not because a form has a name field. AI does the implementation; the human owns architecture, quality, and correctness.

questions.prompt: Agent mode consumes 'premium requests,' and an iterating agent consumes them enthusiastically. The cost-aware habit is:
choices: Avoid agent mode entirely — typing the code yourself remains free of per-request charges
Let the agent iterate freely; compute is cheaper than engineers, per slide 14 of the deck
Submit a request to Finance to reclassify agent traffic as non-premium at the next CAB
Scope tasks tightly and use cheaper modes like ask mode for exploration before going agentic
questions.explanation: Agent mode's self-correcting loop is powerful precisely because it iterates — and every iteration bills a premium request, so a vague task can burn budget while the agent flails. The discipline is to do cheap exploration first (ask mode, reading code yourself, writing a plan) and hand the agent a well-scoped task it can finish in few iterations. 'Compute is cheaper than engineers' fails when unscoped agent runs produce output the engineer must then untangle. Cost-awareness and good context engineering turn out to be the same habit: smaller, sharper tasks.

rounds.title: BOSS: GitHub Copilot in Practice
rounds.boringQuip: Per Directive 9.0, all autonomous agents must file a draft PR, await human sign-off, and remain in-scope at all times. I have reviewed your workflow. It is, regrettably, compliant. Prepare for escalation.
rounds.clearQuip: Assigned the issue, reviewed the draft PR, merged with a colleague's approval. Boring can't even file a complaint — the paperwork was flawless. We're done here.

questions.prompt: Your teammate says 'I used the Copilot agent yesterday' and you have no idea which of the two agentic surfaces they mean. What's the actual difference between agent mode and the coding agent?
choices: Agent mode is the free tier; the coding agent is what you get after your manager approves the budget increase request
They're the same feature — GitHub just renamed it and hoped nobody would ask questions in the all-hands
Agent mode works live in your editor on your current task; the coding agent takes an assigned GitHub issue and works asynchronously in the cloud, returning a draft PR
Agent mode writes code; the coding agent only reviews code written by agent mode
questions.explanation: Copilot has two distinct agentic surfaces: agent mode runs inside VS Code as an autonomous pair programmer on whatever you're doing right now, while the coding agent runs in a GitHub Actions-powered cloud environment — you assign it an issue, walk away, and it comes back with a draft PR. They're not tiers and not a rename; they're synchronous vs. asynchronous ways of working. Rule of thumb: agent mode for work you want to watch, coding agent for backlog items you'd hand to a junior engineer and review later.

questions.prompt: You found a beautifully detailed 2024 tutorial about 'Copilot Workspace.' Before you invest an afternoon in it, what should you know?
choices: Copilot Workspace was sunset in May 2025, and GitHub folded its learnings into the coding agent — the tutorial documents a product that no longer exists
Copilot Workspace is the enterprise-only name for agent mode, so the tutorial applies as long as your org pays for it
Copilot Workspace is still the recommended entry point; the coding agent is its experimental beta channel
Workspace was renamed to VS Code — the tutorial is fine, just mentally substitute the editor
questions.explanation: Copilot Workspace — GitHub's earlier task-to-code environment — was sunset May 30, 2025, with GitHub explicitly rolling its learnings into the coding agent. This matters practically: the AI tooling landscape churns fast enough that a tutorial's publish date is load-bearing information. The 'issue in, draft PR out' workflow it pioneered lives on in the coding agent, so the concepts transfer even though the product didn't.

questions.prompt: You want Copilot to permanently know your repo's conventions ('we use xUnit, never MSTest; run make verify before claiming done'). Where does that repo-wide guidance live?
choices: In a pinned comment on your oldest open issue, where all institutional knowledge traditionally goes to be ignored
In .github/copilot-instructions.md — and the coding agent also reads the tool-agnostic AGENTS.md
You must re-paste it at the start of every chat session; persistent instructions are an enterprise feature
In your personal VS Code settings.json, so every teammate can maintain their own slightly different copy
questions.explanation: Repo-wide custom instructions live in .github/copilot-instructions.md (with path-specific *.instructions.md files for finer scoping), and Copilot's coding agent also honors AGENTS.md — the tool-agnostic standard adopted by 60,000+ projects — plus even CLAUDE.md. That's the whole point of project memory from Module 5: conventions get written down once, in the repo, versioned with the code. Re-pasting per session wastes context and guarantees drift, and per-developer settings files are how you end up with five personal dialects of 'the standard.'

questions.prompt: Your team runs the same release-notes-drafting prompt every sprint, and someone is dutifully copy-pasting it from a Confluence page each time. What's the Copilot-native fix?
choices: Add it to copilot-instructions.md so the release-notes procedure loads into every single request, relevant or not
Create a custom agent for it, since any reusable text is by definition a persona
Schedule a recurring calendar hold titled 'Paste The Prompt' and call it process maturity
Save it as a .prompt.md file — a reusable prompt file you invoke like a slash command instead of copy-pasting
questions.explanation: Prompt files (.prompt.md) encode a repeatable workflow as an invocable command in VS Code, Visual Studio, and JetBrains — the Copilot analog of Claude Code's custom slash commands. The tempting wrong answer is stuffing it into copilot-instructions.md, but that file is for always-relevant project context; a task you run occasionally would just bloat every request's context window. The division of labor: instructions files hold what's always true, prompt files hold what you sometimes do.

questions.prompt: You want a dedicated 'security reviewer' persona in Copilot — its own instructions, its own scoped tools, summoned by name. What's the mechanism?
choices: A custom agent defined in a .agent.md file (creatable via /create-agent) — a scoped persona with its own instructions and tools
Open a second VS Code window, adopt a stern tone in chat, and hope the model infers the role from context
Custom personas require standing up your own MCP server; Copilot has no built-in concept of scoped agents
Rename your copilot-instructions.md to security-instructions.md on a dedicated review branch
questions.explanation: Custom agents live in .agent.md files — Copilot's version of the subagent concept from Module 6: a named, scoped persona with its own instructions and tool access, and /create-agent will scaffold one for you. MCP is the wrong tool here — it connects agents to external tools and data, it doesn't define personas. The practical win of a scoped agent is isolation: your security reviewer stays focused on its checklist instead of inheriting the full clutter of your main session's context.

questions.prompt: You want Copilot to query your team's internal ticket system directly. GitHub used to have 'Copilot Extensions' for this sort of thing. What's the current story?
choices: Copilot Extensions remain the blessed path; write one per tool per editor and budget a quarter for the marketplace review
Copilot can only touch GitHub-owned data sources; external systems require exporting everything to CSV like our ancestors did
GitHub deprecated proprietary Copilot Extensions in favor of MCP — an open standard, with VS Code implementing the full MCP specification
You install a browser extension that screenshots the ticket system and pastes the image into chat
questions.explanation: GitHub retired its proprietary Copilot Extensions and went all-in on the Model Context Protocol — the open standard from Module 6 where servers expose tools, resources, and prompts to any compatible client, and VS Code implements the full spec. This is the transferability payoff: an MCP server you configure for Copilot works with Claude Code, Codex, and other MCP clients too. One protocol instead of one bespoke integration per tool per vendor — the rare case where the industry actually agreed on something.

questions.prompt: You're staring at Copilot's mode picker — Ask, Edit, Plan, Agent — with a gnarly refactor you don't fully understand yet. Per the recommended workflow, where do you start?
choices: Agent mode immediately — the whole point of autonomy is not having to understand your own request first
Ask mode to scope and understand the problem, then switch to agent mode for execution
Edit mode, because a refactor technically edits files, and technicalities are how decisions get made
Alternate modes every prompt so that no single mode accumulates too much organizational influence
questions.explanation: The guidance is 'start with ask mode to scope the problem, then switch to agent mode for execution' — which is just Module 7's research-plan-implement loop wearing a Copilot badge. Jumping straight to agent mode on a task you can't yet describe precisely is how you get confident multi-file changes to the wrong thing. Ask mode is also cheap exploration; agent mode's autonomous iteration is where the premium requests go, so scoping first is good engineering and good economics.

questions.prompt: A teammate fires off vague agent-mode requests all day and is shocked when the team's 'premium requests' allowance evaporates. What's the actual cost-awareness lesson?
choices: Premium requests are unlimited on paid plans; the counter exists purely to build character
Only the cloud coding agent consumes premium requests; in-editor agent mode runs locally on your laptop's own goodwill
The fix is shorter prompts — billing is per word typed, so vagueness is actually the thrifty option
Agent mode and the coding agent iterate autonomously and burn premium requests fast — so scope tasks tightly and use ask mode for cheap exploration first
questions.explanation: Both agentic surfaces consume premium requests, and because agents loop autonomously — editing, running, checking, retrying — a single vague ask can fan out into many billable iterations. The economical workflow mirrors the effective one: explore in ask mode, hand the agent a tight well-scoped task, and you pay for productive iteration instead of expensive wandering. Cost-awareness isn't stinginess; a precisely scoped task is cheaper and better simultaneously.

questions.prompt: You assign the coding agent an issue, it opens a lovely draft PR, and you — the assigner — reach for the Approve button. What happens, per GitHub's human-in-the-loop guardrails?
choices: You can't be the approver — the developer who asked the agent to open the PR cannot be the one to approve it; another human must review
You can approve it, but a modal asks if you're really sure, which legally constitutes oversight
The agent approves its own PR once its self-tests pass, freeing humans for higher-value meetings
Approval routes automatically to whoever last touched the affected files, per git blame, as tradition demands
questions.explanation: GitHub's docs are explicit: the developer who asks the agent to open a pull request cannot be the one to approve it — the same author-can't-approve separation you'd apply to human PRs, now applied to your robot proxy. The agent itself can't approve or merge anything either. This is Module 8's core rule made structural: 'never assume generated code is production-ready' works best when the review requirement is enforced by the platform rather than by everyone's discipline on a Friday afternoon.

questions.prompt: Beyond mandatory review, the coding agent's execution environment has its own containment rules. Which of these is actually true of how it runs?
choices: It runs with your full developer credentials on your machine, because trust is the foundation of any healthy team
It has unrestricted internet access, since limiting a cloud agent's connectivity would hurt its feelings and its benchmarks
It runs in an isolated GitHub Actions environment with internet access limited to trusted destinations, and Actions workflows won't run without your approval
It runs wherever compute is cheapest that hour; the location is disclosed quarterly in the compliance report
questions.explanation: The coding agent runs in an isolated GitHub Actions-powered environment, its internet access is tightly limited to a trusted allowlist, its output gets scanned by CodeQL and secret scanning, and workflows won't execute without your approval. These layers exist for Module 8 reasons: limited egress blunts data exfiltration if the agent is prompt-injected, and isolation means a misbehaving run can't touch your machine or credentials. Sandboxing isn't distrust of the tool — it's the same blast-radius thinking you'd apply to any automated system with write access.
